title: Toba Caldera VEI-8 Eruption: Magma Chamber Collapse and Climatic Consequences

lesson.learn: On June 6, approximately 74,000 years ago, a large-scale VEI-8 eruption occurred at the Toba caldera, releasing approximately 2,800 cubic kilometers of rhyolitic magma into the atmosphere.
lesson.simpler: The Toba caldera experienced a catastrophic VEI-8 eruption, resulting in a calderic collapse and pyroclastic flow.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The Toba caldera eruption demonstrates the significant impact of a massive magma chamber collapse on global atmospheric circulation and climatic systems.
lesson.deeperDive: Studies have identified changes in Earth's rotation axis, gravity measurements, and seismic data as evidence of a massive gravitational collapse of the volcano's magma chamber, triggering the eruption. An estimated 200 billion metric tons of sulfur dioxide were released into the atmosphere, leading to a stratospheric aerosol injection that persisted for approximately 9 years. The eruption caused a global temperature drop of approximately 13°C (23°F) for several years, affecting atmospheric circulation patterns, including a weakened Asian summer monsoon. Additionally, the Toba caldera eruption has been linked to mass extinction events.
lesson.funFact: The eruption released an estimated 200 billion metric tons of sulfur dioxide into the atmosphere, resulting in a stratospheric aerosol injection that persisted for 9 years.

years: 74,000 years ago: The last major VEI-8 eruption occurred at Toba Caldera
